summary: DHH describes Basecamp's decision to temporarily remove the 'claps' (likes/applause) feature from their own internal Basecamp account to better understand its impact. He noticed personal anxiety around the feature — worrying about whether selectively applauding some updates but not others sent unintended signals about favoritism or dissatisfaction. Rather than endlessly debating improvements, they simply killed the feature for a six-week cycle. The experiment immediately surfaced strong opinions from the team and revealed that people started writing more substantive comments without the easy out of a quick clap.
key_points: Likes and claps in collaboration tools create subtle anxiety about perceived favoritism and implicit judgment when not applied equally
Rather than brainstorming the perfect solution to a fuzzy problem, sometimes the best approach is to simply remove the feature and observe what happens
Removing claps led to more substantive commenting — the easy-out reaction had been suppressing deeper engagement
Dogfooding product changes on your own team before deciding for customers provides real insight that abstract discussion cannot
money_quotes: If I applauded an update from Sam yesterday, but don't today, does that mean I'm expressing discontent with the most recent work?
Frequently my answer to this question was: Applaud equally for everyone or no one. An easy out for anxiety, but not actually very helpful.
So instead of trying ever-harder to think up the perfect solution, we grabbed the hammer instead and decided to whack the feature.
No big discussion, no brainstorming, no endless contemplation. Just a big whack, and let's see where this goes.
Hey, isn't it interesting that we're all commenting with a lot more depth now that the easy out of a clap isn't there any more?
When in doubt, give it a whack.
key_insight: When a feature creates more anxiety than value, the fastest path to clarity is removing it entirely and observing what changes, rather than endlessly debating improvements.
